title: Payroll Compliance for Small Business in Jamaica — Under 10 Employees Guide
subtitle: Everything Jamaican micro-businesses need to know about payroll — registration, deductions, filing, and how to stay compliant without an HR department

ctaInline.headline: Payroll Made Simple for Small Teams
ctaInline.text: Calculate your first payslip free — no accounting degree needed

ctaBottom.headline: Ready to get your small business payroll right?
ctaBottom.text: Start your free trial — plans from J$3,500/month for small teams

html:
You started a business in Jamaica. Maybe it's a restaurant, a small shop, a landscaping crew, a beauty salon, or a professional services firm. You've hired a few people — under 10 — and now you need to pay them. Properly. Legally. Without getting a letter from Tax Administration Jamaica (TAJ) that ruins your week.

Here's the reality: Jamaica's payroll compliance requirements apply to you from the moment you hire your first employee. There is no exemption for small businesses. There is no "too small to file" threshold. Whether you employ one person or one thousand, you must register with the relevant authorities, calculate statutory deductions correctly, remit payments on time, and maintain records that can survive an audit.

The good news? It's not as complicated as it sounds — once you understand what's required. This guide explains everything in plain language, specifically for Jamaican businesses with fewer than 10 employees. No jargon. No assumptions that you have an accountant on staff. Just what you need to know and what you need to do.

Step 1: Register as an Employer

Before you process your first payroll, you need to be registered with three government agencies. This isn't optional, and doing payroll without these registrations is illegal.

Tax Registration Number (TRN) for Business

If you haven't already, register your business with TAJ and obtain a business TRN. This is separate from your personal TRN. If you're a sole trader, you'll use your personal TRN but must still register as an employer. Visit any TAJ office or apply online at TAJ's website. You'll need your business registration documents, a valid ID, and proof of business address.

Employer Registration with TAJ

Once you have your business TRN, register specifically as an employer with TAJ. This activates your obligation to withhold PAYE (income tax) and Education Tax from employees' pay and remit those amounts to TAJ monthly. TAJ will issue you an employer reference number that you'll use on all your monthly and annual filings.

NIS and NHT Registration

Register separately with the National Insurance Scheme (NIS) and the National Housing Trust (NHT). NIS registration is done through the Ministry of Labour, and NHT registration through the NHT offices. Both agencies will assign you employer reference numbers. Your employees also need individual NIS numbers — if a new hire doesn't have one, you'll need to help them register before you can process their first payroll.

All three registrations should be completed before your first pay date. Processing payroll without proper registration doesn't defer your obligations — it just means you'll owe back-payments plus penalties when TAJ catches up with you. And they will.

Step 2: Understand Your Four Statutory Deductions

Every Jamaican employer must calculate and remit four statutory deductions. Here's exactly what each one is, who pays it, and how much.

PAYE — Pay As You Earn (Income Tax)

PAYE is Jamaica's income tax system for employed persons. You deduct it from your employee's pay and send it to TAJ.

How it works: Every employee gets a tax-free allowance of J$1,500,096 per year, which works out to J$125,008 per month. If your employee earns less than J$125,008 per month, they pay zero PAYE. If they earn more, you deduct 25% on the amount above J$125,008 (up to J$500,000 per month), and 30% on anything above J$500,000 per month.

Example: An employee earning J$200,000/month has taxable income of J$200,000 minus J$125,008 = J$74,992. PAYE deduction: J$74,992 x 25% = J$18,748 per month.

Who pays: The employee pays — you deduct it from their gross pay. But you are responsible for calculating it correctly and sending it to TAJ. If you get it wrong, TAJ comes after you, not the employee.

NIS — National Insurance Scheme

NIS funds pensions, sickness benefits, and maternity benefits for Jamaican workers.

How it works: Both you and your employee each pay 3% of the employee's gross earnings. There's a ceiling: once an employee's cumulative annual earnings reach J$5,000,000, NIS deductions stop for the rest of the tax year. For most small business employees, this ceiling won't apply — it only kicks in for employees earning roughly J$417,000 or more per month.

Example: An employee earning J$200,000/month pays J$6,000 in NIS (3%). You also pay J$6,000 as the employer contribution. Total NIS for that employee: J$12,000/month.

Who pays: Both. You deduct the employee's 3% from their pay, and you pay an additional 3% from your own pocket as the employer. Both amounts are remitted together.

NHT — National Housing Trust

NHT funds Jamaica's national housing programme and gives employees access to housing loans and refunds.

How it works: Employees pay 2% of gross earnings. Employers pay 3% of the employee's gross earnings. There is no ceiling — NHT applies on the full amount regardless of how much the employee earns.

Example: An employee earning J$200,000/month pays J$4,000 in NHT (2%). You pay J$6,000 as the employer contribution (3%). Total NHT: J$10,000/month.

Who pays: Both, at different rates. You deduct the employee's 2% and pay your own 3%.

Education Tax

Education Tax funds Jamaica's education system. It's the deduction most small business owners forget about — or miscalculate.

How it works: Employees pay 2.25% of gross earnings. Employers pay 3.5% of the employee's gross earnings. There is no threshold and no ceiling — it applies from the first dollar.

Example: An employee earning J$200,000/month pays J$4,500 in Education Tax (2.25%). You pay J$7,000 as the employer contribution (3.5%). Total Education Tax: J$11,500/month.

Who pays: Both, at different rates. This is where many small businesses make errors — they either forget the employer portion entirely or apply the wrong rate.

Step 3: Calculate a Complete Payslip

Let's put it all together with a real example. Say you employ a shop assistant earning J$200,000 gross per month.

Employee deductions (taken from their pay):

  • PAYE: J$18,748 (25% of J$74,992 — the amount above the J$125,008 monthly threshold)
  • NIS: J$6,000 (3% of J$200,000)
  • NHT: J$4,000 (2% of J$200,000)
  • Education Tax: J$4,500 (2.25% of J$200,000)
  • Total deductions: J$33,248
  • Net pay (take-home): J$166,752

Employer costs (additional amounts you pay):

  • NIS (employer): J$6,000 (3%)
  • NHT (employer): J$6,000 (3%)
  • Education Tax (employer): J$7,000 (3.5%)
  • Total employer statutory costs: J$19,000

So for an employee with a J$200,000 gross salary, your total cost is J$219,000 per month — the salary plus your employer contributions. This is the number that should be in your budget, not just the gross salary figure.

Don't want to do this calculation by hand? Use our free Jamaica payroll calculator to get an instant, accurate breakdown for any salary amount. It calculates all four statutory deductions automatically — no spreadsheet required.

Step 4: Know Your Filing Deadlines

Missing a deadline is the fastest way to turn a small payroll obligation into an expensive one. Here are the deadlines every Jamaican small business must meet:

Monthly: All statutory deductions (PAYE, NIS, NHT, Education Tax) must be remitted to TAJ and the respective agencies by the 14th of the following month. Pay your employees on January 31? Your statutory payments are due by February 14. Late payments attract penalties starting at J$5,000 per day.

Annually: You must file an annual return with TAJ summarizing all employee earnings and deductions for the tax year (April 1 to March 31). This is due by March 14 of the following year — but smart employers prepare it as soon as the tax year closes on March 31.

On termination: When an employee leaves — whether they resign, are terminated, or their contract ends — you must provide them with a P45 showing their total earnings and deductions for the tax year to date. This must be filed with TAJ within 14 days of the termination date.

Step 5: Stop Using Excel for Payroll

Let's be honest: most Jamaican small businesses with under 10 employees are doing payroll in Excel. Some are doing it on paper. A few are doing it in their heads. All of these approaches have the same problem — they work until they don't, and when they fail, the failure is expensive.

Here's what goes wrong with manual payroll:

Rate changes: When TAJ updates the PAYE threshold (which happened recently with the move to J$1,500,096), you have to remember to update your spreadsheet. If you don't, every payslip from that point forward is wrong — and you owe back-payments.

Calculation errors: A misplaced decimal point in an NIS calculation doesn't just affect one payslip — it compounds every month until you catch it. With 5 employees over 12 months, that's 60 opportunities for a single formula error to create a compliance problem.

No audit trail: When TAJ audits your payroll — and small businesses do get audited — they want to see records, not a spreadsheet that could have been modified last week. A proper payroll system maintains timestamped, unalterable records of every calculation.

Employee disputes: When an employee questions their deductions, can you show them exactly how each number was calculated? With Excel, the answer is usually "let me check the formula" — which doesn't inspire confidence.

How PayrollJamaica Makes Small Business Payroll Affordable

PayrollJamaica was built for Jamaican businesses of every size — including the shop with 3 employees and the startup with 7. Our small business plan starts at J$3,500/month, which covers:

Automatic statutory calculations: PAYE, NIS, NHT, and Education Tax — all calculated correctly, every cycle, using the latest TAJ rates. No manual updates. No spreadsheet formulas to debug.

Payslip generation: Professional, detailed payslips for every employee showing gross pay, each statutory deduction, and net pay. Your employees can download them anytime through our self-service portal.

TAJ-ready reports: Monthly filing summaries and annual returns generated automatically in the format TAJ requires. When filing day comes, your report is ready — you just submit it.

Bank payment files: Generate direct deposit files for NCB, JN Bank, Scotiabank Jamaica, and Sagicor in one click. Stop writing individual cheques or making manual bank transfers for each employee.

Filing reminders: Automated notifications before every deadline so you never miss a remittance date. The J$5,000/day late penalty is easily avoided when your system tells you the 14th is coming.

For a business with 5 employees, J$3,500/month for payroll compliance is less than what you'd pay an accountant for a single hour of work to sort out a TAJ penalty. It's less than the cost of one late-filing day. It's the most affordable insurance policy your small business can buy.

Getting Started: Your First 30 Minutes

Here's how to go from manual payroll to fully compliant automated payroll in under 30 minutes:

  1. Run a free calculation: Use our Jamaica payroll calculator to see exactly what your employees' payslips should look like. Compare the results against what you've been calculating manually — you might be surprised.
  2. Sign up for a free trial: No credit card needed. Enter your business details and your employees' basic information (name, TRN, NIS number, salary).
  3. Run your first payroll: Process a test payroll cycle and review every payslip. Compare against your manual calculations. See the difference that automated statutory compliance makes.
  4. Go live: Connect your bank, process your first real payroll, and never worry about PAYE thresholds, NIS ceilings, or filing deadlines again.

Explore our full payroll software features to see everything that's included — even at the small business tier.

Your business might be small. Your payroll obligations aren't. But meeting them doesn't have to be hard, expensive, or stressful. PayrollJamaica gives you the same compliance tools that Jamaica's largest employers use — at a price that makes sense for a team of 10 or fewer.

Start today. Your first payslip calculation is free.
